document_title: The Effects of Non-Nutritive Artificial Sweeteners, Aspartame and Sucralose, on the Gut Microbiome in Healthy Adults: Secondary Outcomes of a Randomized Double-Blinded Crossover Clinical Trial
authors: Samar Y. Ahmad, James Friel, Dylan Mackay

test_cases.query: What were the main findings regarding gut microbiota composition after aspartame and sucralose consumption in this trial?
test_cases.ground_truth_answer: There were no differences in the median relative proportions of the most abundant bacterial taxa at family and genus level before and after treatments with both NNSs. The microbiota community structure also did not show any obvious differences (PERMANOVA p = 0.99). These findings suggest that daily repeated consumption of pure aspartame or sucralose at realistic doses has minimal effect on gut microbiota composition.

test_cases.query: What doses of aspartame and sucralose were used in the intervention, and what percentage of the ADI do they represent?
test_cases.ground_truth_answer: The doses used were 0.425 g/day of aspartame (representing 14% of the acceptable daily intake) and 0.136 g/day of sucralose (representing 20% of the ADI). These doses reflect the intake of consumers who drink approximately three 355 mL cans of diet soda per day.

test_cases.query: What were the inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial?
test_cases.ground_truth_answer: Participants were required to be healthy, non-diabetic adults aged 18–45 years with a BMI of 20–25 (normal weight), fasting blood glucose below 5.7 mmol/L, and non-consumers of NNSs. Women with regular menstrual cycles who were not taking oral contraceptive pills were included, and they all began the study at approximately the follicular phase of their cycle.

test_cases.query: What was the overall study design, including duration and washout period?
test_cases.ground_truth_answer: The study was a randomized, double-blind, crossover controlled clinical trial. Participants underwent a 4-week baseline period with no NNS consumption, followed by a 2-week treatment period (weeks 5–6), a 4-week washout period (weeks 7–10), and a second 2-week treatment period (weeks 11–12) with the alternate sweetener. The total study duration was 12 weeks.

test_cases.query: How many participants completed the study, and what was their baseline demographic profile?
test_cases.ground_truth_answer: Seventeen participants completed the study (10 females and 7 males). Their mean age was 24 ± 6.8 years, mean BMI was 22.9 ± 2.5 kg/m², and fasting blood glucose was 5.3 ± 0.1 mmol/L. All participants were young healthy adults with biochemical markers within the normal range. Two participants from the first group dropped out without providing a reason.

test_cases.query: How were fecal samples collected, processed, and analyzed for microbiome characterization?
test_cases.ground_truth_answer: Participants collected fecal samples at days 1, 28, 42, and 84, sampling from three random sites of the stool into sterile tubes kept at ~4°C. Samples were delivered within 6–8 hours and stored at −80°C. Bacterial DNA was extracted using the MagAttract PowerSoil DNA KF Kit on a KingFisher robot with bead-beating for mechanical lysis. The 16S rRNA V4 region was PCR-amplified with dual-barcoded primers and sequenced on the Illumina MiSeq platform (300-bp paired-end). Sequences were classified using Greengenes v13_8 at 97% OTU similarity using mothur software, yielding a mean of 28,802 quality-filtered reads per sample.

test_cases.query: What statistical methods were used to assess alpha diversity and beta diversity in this study?
test_cases.ground_truth_answer: Alpha diversity was estimated using the Shannon index on raw OTU abundance tables after contaminant removal, tested with a linear mixed-effects model. Beta diversity was computed using Bray–Curtis dissimilarity indices after excluding OTUs with fewer than 3 counts in at least 10% of samples, and visualized using Principal Coordinate Analysis (PCoA). Significant variation in beta diversity was tested using Permutational Analysis of Variance (PERMANOVA / Adonis R function) with 9,999 permutations. Differential abundance testing was performed using DESeq2.

test_cases.query: How were short-chain fatty acids extracted from fecal samples and what analytical method was used for detection?
test_cases.ground_truth_answer: Fecal suspension was prepared in Milli-Q-grade water and homogenized using MP Bio Fast Prep for 1 minute at 4.0 m/s. Samples were acidified to pH 2.0 using 5M HCl, then centrifuged at 10,000 RPM to isolate the supernatant, which was diluted to a final concentration of 1 mM and stored in 2 mL GC vials. Detection was performed by gas chromatography (Thermo Trace 1310) coupled to a flame ionization detector, using a Thermo TG-WAXMS A GC column (30 m, 0.32 mm, 0.25 µm).

test_cases.query: What role do SCFAs play in human physiology, and why were they measured in this study?
test_cases.ground_truth_answer: SCFAs are the main end-products of fermentation of non-digestible carbohydrates by gut microbiota, including acetate, butyrate, formate, and propionate. They play important roles in glucose metabolism, lipid metabolism, appetite regulation, and immune system function. Since any change in gut microbiota composition could potentially alter SCFA production, and because NNSs were hypothesized to affect microbial activity, measuring SCFAs served as a functional readout of microbiota metabolic output alongside compositional analysis.

test_cases.query: What dietary restrictions were placed on participants during the study period, and what is the rationale for each?
test_cases.ground_truth_answer: Participants were instructed to avoid all NNSs (including acesulfame potassium, saccharin, stevia, monk fruit) to prevent confounding. Caffeinated beverage intake was limited to two cups per day because caffeine affects glucose uptake rates. Alcohol was capped at two units per day due to its effects on blood glucose, insulin, and the gut microbiome. Probiotic supplements and probiotic-rich foods such as yogurt, kefir, kombucha, and tempeh were prohibited as they directly alter microbiota composition. Ibuprofen was banned because NSAIDs affect gut microbiota function and composition, while acetaminophen was permitted as an alternative.

test_cases.query: What was the significance of the sequence factor in the PERMANOVA analysis, and how did the authors interpret it?
test_cases.ground_truth_answer: The PERMANOVA Adonis analysis found a statistically significant difference in beta diversity based on the sequence factor (p = 0.02, R² = 0.03), meaning the order in which participants received the NNS treatments (aspartame-first vs. sucralose-first) influenced beta diversity. However, no significant differences were detected for the type of treatment received (p = 0.99) or the period of treatment (p = 0.78). The authors interpreted this sequence effect as suggesting that the order of NNS receipt, rather than the identity of the NNS itself, influenced microbiome community structure, though the R² value indicates this explained only 3% of variation.

test_cases.query: How do the findings of this human trial contrast with animal model studies on NNS effects on the gut microbiome, and what methodological factors explain the discrepancy?
test_cases.ground_truth_answer: Animal studies have demonstrated that NNSs including sucralose and aspartame can alter bacterial genera and SCFA profiles. For example, studies in rats and mice showed that sucralose (Splenda) alters gut microflora, increases intestinal P-glycoprotein and cytochrome P-450, and that low-dose aspartame differentially affects gut microbiota-host metabolic interactions in diet-induced obese rats. In contrast, this human crossover trial found no measurable changes in microbiota composition, diversity, or SCFA levels. The authors caution that animal data are often a poor predictor of human response, and differences in the chemical structures and metabolic fates of NNSs between species, as well as the doses used, likely explain the divergent findings. The study also used pure NNS powders, isolating the sweetener effect from other soda ingredients.

test_cases.query: How does this study's finding on saccharin differ from the prior Suez et al. (2014) study, and what limitations prevent direct comparison?
test_cases.ground_truth_answer: Suez et al. (2014) reported that 4 out of 7 healthy subjects who consumed saccharin at the FDA's maximal ADI for 5 days showed poorer glycaemic responses, with pronounced microbiome compositional changes linked to glucose intolerance — confirmed via fecal transplant into germ-free mice. The current study found no microbiota changes with aspartame or sucralose. The authors note that Suez et al. used saccharin above the ADI, while this study used doses below 20% of ADI for different sweeteners. The different chemical structures, metabolic fates, dose levels, and NNS identities mean the Suez saccharin findings cannot be extrapolated to aspartame or sucralose.

test_cases.query: What are the key limitations of this study that could affect the interpretation of the null microbiome findings?
test_cases.ground_truth_answer: Several limitations affect interpretation: (1) The 14-day intervention period may be too short to detect meaningful microbiome changes, though it was constrained by the crossover design and the need to minimize dropout rates. (2) The study was primarily powered to detect effects on blood glucose metabolism, not gut microbiome changes, making the microbiome analysis an underpowered exploratory outcome. (3) The small sample size (n=17) further limits statistical power for microbiome endpoints. (4) The 16S rRNA V4 amplicon approach has inherent taxonomic resolution limits compared to shotgun metagenomics. Together, these suggest the null findings should be interpreted as 'no detectable effect at this dose and duration' rather than conclusive evidence of complete microbiome inertness.

test_cases.query: What are the theoretical mechanisms by which NNSs could potentially alter gut microbiota, and how does sucralose's metabolic fate in the body relate to its microbiome impact?
test_cases.ground_truth_answer: NNSs could potentially alter gut microbiota through two main mechanisms: (1) Some gut microbes may have the ability to metabolize NNSs, causing a shift in normal bacterial balance. (2) NNSs may exert a bacteriostatic effect on certain gut microbes, directly inhibiting their growth and changing microbiome composition. Regarding sucralose specifically, multiple pharmacokinetic studies in animals and humans have consistently shown that sucralose is not absorbed but is eliminated unchanged in feces, making it unlikely to serve as a substrate for gut microbiota. This metabolic inertness provides a mechanistic basis for why sucralose may not alter the microbiome, and is consistent with the null findings in this trial.

mcq_questions.question: What was the acceptable daily intake (ADI) of sucralose as defined by Health Canada in this study?
mcq_questions.explanation: Health Canada defined the ADI of sucralose as 9 mg/kg body weight, while the ADI for aspartame was 40 mg/kg body weight.

mcq_questions.question: How many faecal samples were analysed in total across all 17 participants in this study?
mcq_questions.explanation: A total of 64 fecal samples were analyzed — four samples per participant (two baselines and two endpoints) across 17 participants, with four missing samples at different timepoints.

mcq_questions.question: What were the two dominant bacterial phyla that together comprised approximately 90% of the adult gut microbiota, as described in this paper?
mcq_questions.explanation: According to the paper, Bacteroidetes and Firmicutes are the two bacterial phyla that dominate almost 90% of the adult gut microbiota. Proteobacteria, Verrucomicrobia, Fusobacteria, and Cyanobacteria represent minority populations.

mcq_questions.question: What was the mean bottle count adherence for the aspartame treatment group?
mcq_questions.explanation: The mean bottle count adherence for the aspartame treatment was 99.47 ± 1.49%, compared to 100% for the sucralose treatment, indicating excellent compliance in both groups.

mcq_questions.question: What was the PERMANOVA p-value for the 'Treatment' factor in the Adonis analysis of microbiota community structure?
mcq_questions.explanation: The Adonis PERMANOVA analysis showed a p-value of 0.99 for the Treatment factor (R² = 0.012), confirming no significant difference in overall microbiota community structure between aspartame and sucralose treatments. The only significant factor was Sequence (p = 0.02).

mcq_questions.question: Which molecular target region of the 16S rRNA gene was amplified for microbiome profiling in this study?
mcq_questions.explanation: Bacterial 16S rRNA genes were PCR-amplified using dual-barcoded primers targeting the V4 region (primers 515F and 806R) according to the protocol of Kozich et al. (2013), followed by 300-bp paired-end sequencing on the Illumina MiSeq platform.

mcq_questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the Shannon diversity index results before and after NNS treatments?
options: A) Shannon index increased significantly after both aspartame and sucralose treatment
B) Shannon index decreased significantly after sucralose but not aspartame treatment
C) Shannon index showed no significant differences before or after treatment with either NNS
D) Shannon index decreased after aspartame treatment only (p < 0.05)
mcq_questions.explanation: The gut microbiota richness and evenness as measured by the Shannon index did not change following 14 days of aspartame or sucralose treatment. For sucralose: pre 4.25 ± 0.79 vs post 4.18 ± 0.81 (p = 0.63); for aspartame: pre 4.08 ± 0.82 vs post 4.07 ± 0.65 (p = 0.96).

mcq_questions.question: What was the number of observed species in the study's OTU dataset?
mcq_questions.explanation: The number of observed species in the dataset was 245. The resulting OTU database contained 55,886 OTUs in total, with an average of 28,802 quality-filtered reads generated per sample.

mcq_questions.question: In the Suez et al. (2014) saccharin study cited in this paper, which experimental approach was used to confirm that gut microbiota dysbiosis caused glucose intolerance rather than the sweetener acting directly?
options: A) Antibiotic treatment to deplete gut bacteria in the original participants
B) In vitro incubation of saccharin with isolated bacterial cultures
C) Transfer of fecal microbiota from affected participants into germ-free mice
D) 16S rRNA sequencing comparing pre- and post-saccharin microbiomes
mcq_questions.explanation: Suez et al. demonstrated causality by transferring fecal microbiota from the participants who developed glucose intolerance after saccharin consumption into germ-free mice. The recipient mice also developed glucose intolerance, confirming that the microbiota changes — not the sweetener directly — mediated the metabolic effect.

mcq_questions.question: Which two bacterial taxa showed the most statistically significant differential abundance in the DESeq2 analysis performed in this study?
options: A) Actinobacteria (genus Bifidobacterium) and Proteobacteria (order Burkholderiales)
B) Bacteroidetes (genus Bacteroides, unclassified) and Firmicutes (order Clostridiales, unclassified)
C) Verrucomicrobia (genus Akkermansia) and Bacteroidetes (genus Prevotella)
D) Firmicutes (genus Faecalibacterium) and Actinobacteria (genus Collinsella)
mcq_questions.explanation: Two OTUs were identified during DESeq2 differential abundance testing. The two bacterial phyla most differentially abundant among treatments were Bacteroidetes (genus Bacteroides, unclassified) and Firmicutes (order Clostridiales, unclassified), both with Padj < 0.05. Notably, this differential abundance was detected despite no overall community-level differences in PERMANOVA.
